title: Inventory Costing: FIFO vs LIFO vs Weighted Average
event: Reyes Co. has beginning inventory of 10 units @ $10, then purchases 20 units @ $12 and 10 units @ $14 (goods available: 40 units, $480). It sells 25 units. Compute cost of goods sold and ending inventory under the assumed method (periodic).

computationPaths.narration: FIFO sells the OLDEST layers first — so ending inventory holds the newest costs. With rising prices: lowest COGS, highest income.
LIFO sells the NEWEST layers first — ending inventory keeps the oldest costs. With rising prices: highest COGS, lowest income (and lowest taxes).
Weighted average blends every unit into one cost, then applies it to both sold and unsold units.

memorize.body: FIFO = First-In, First-Out → OLD costs to COGS, NEW costs stay in inventory. LIFO flips it.
COGS + Ending inventory ALWAYS = goods available. Check every answer against it — here, everything sums to $480.
Rising prices: FIFO → lower COGS, higher income. LIFO → higher COGS, lower income, lower taxes. Exams test the DIRECTION as much as the math.

questions.prompt: Using the data above, FIFO cost of goods sold = ?

distractors.feedback: That's LIFO — you sold the newest layers first. FIFO sends the OLDEST costs to COGS: 10 @ $10 + 15 @ $12 = 280.
That's weighted average (25 × $12). FIFO uses actual layers, oldest first.
That's FIFO's ENDING INVENTORY. The question asked for COGS — the other half of the $480.
